plainMeaning: This verse paints the heavenly women who honour the Jina—gliding through the open sky with the grace of swans, full and lovely of form, their eyes wide like lotus petals.
recitation: aṁbaraṁtara viāraṇiāāhiṁ, lalia haṁsavahu gāmiṇiāāhiṁ. pīṇa soṇithaṇa sāliṇiāāhiṁ, sakala kamaladala loaṇiāāhiṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: within the sky, through the interior of the firmament
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ambara ('sky, firmament') + antara ('interior, the space within'); here adverbial/locative — 'inside the open sky'.
wordByWord.notes: Sets the aerial stage: the celestial women move on high, not on earth.

wordByWord.gloss: by [those women] coursing / cleaving through, ranging about
wordByWord.etymology: From Pkt. viyaraṇa / Skt. vicaraṇa ('roaming, coursing'); fem. instrumental plural in -iāāhiṁ (Apabhraṁśa instr. pl., from Skt. -ābhiḥ).
wordByWord.notes: First of four parallel instrumental-plural epithets; all hang on the act of veneration carried across the surrounding devasundarī stanzas.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. lalita ('graceful, charming'); Pkt. lalia by intervocalic softening of -t-.
Skt. haṁsa ('swan, goose') + vadhū ('young woman, bride') → Pkt. vahu; 'swan-brides'.
wordByWord.notes: A simile-in-compound: their gait is that of graceful she-swans.

wordByWord.gloss: by [those women] of [such] gait, moving / stepping [so]
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. gāminī ('a female who goes, having a gait'), from √gam ('to go'); fem. instr. pl. -iāāhiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Reads with the previous two tokens: 'with the lovely gait of swan-maidens'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pīna ('full, rounded, fleshy'); unchanged in Pkt.
Skt. śroṇi ('hips, loins') → Pkt. soṇi + Skt. stana ('breast') → Pkt. thaṇa; dvandva compound.
wordByWord.notes: Printed conjoined in the book; with pīṇa = 'full of hip and breast', the classical ideal feminine form.

wordByWord.gloss: by [those women] graced with, endowed with
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śālinī ('possessing, graced with, adorned by'), from √śal; fem. instr. pl. -iāāhiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: 'Graced with full hips and breasts' — third instrumental epithet.
Here 'a whole / fully unfurled' lotus-petal, conveying wide, open eyes.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. kamala ('lotus') + dala ('petal, leaf'); tatpuruṣa compound.
wordByWord.notes: Printed conjoined; supplies the standard simile for the eyes.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. locana ('eye') → Pkt. loaṇa (loss of intervocalic -c-); fem. instr. pl. -iāāhiṁ, a bahuvrīhi ('having eyes [like] full lotus-petals').
wordByWord.notes: Fourth and closing instrumental epithet of the quatrain.

literalTranslation: By [the celestial women] coursing through the open sky; by those with the lovely gait of swan-maidens; by those graced with full hips and breasts; by those whose eyes are [like] fully-opened lotus-petals —
idiomaticTranslation: [The Jina's feet are worshipped] by celestial women who cleave gracefully through the open sky, gliding with the lovely step of swan-maidens; full and shapely of form, their wide eyes like petals of an unfurled lotus.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The whole stanza is a single, breathless garland of four feminine instrumental-plural epithets, each ending in the chiming rhyme -iāāhiṁ — a deliberate sonic device that makes the line shimmer when chanted and mimics, in sound, the gliding motion it describes. Grammatically nothing is finite here: the four descriptors all hang, in the instrumental, on the act of veneration that runs across the neighbouring stanzas (the women bowing at the Jina's invincible feet). Read it as four brushstrokes of one portrait. First their element and motion — aerial, sky-coursing, like she-swans (haṁsavahu), the swan being the classic emblem of effortless grace. Then their form — pīṇa-soṇi-thaṇa-sāliṇī, full of hip and breast — and finally their gaze — sakala-kamaladala-loaṇa, eyes wide as a wholly opened lotus. The verse advances the tableau by tightening focus: where surrounding stanzas place the heavenly assembly in motion and song, verse 26 zooms to the bodily grace of the devasundarī, and the following verse will pile on their ornaments — girdle-bells, anklets, jewelled armlets. Note the poet's restraint: he names beauty in stock similes (swan, lotus) precisely so that it remains decorative, a foil — all this loveliness is in the act of bowing, not the object of the verse's reverence.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The lavish sensual portrait is not indulgence but a hierarchy-of-glory device. The most beautiful women of the heavens — devas who, however exalted, are still bound within saṁsāra — turn their grace toward the Tīrthaṅkara's feet. Their beauty is thus subordinated: it magnifies the Jina's stature rather than soliciting desire. The Jina, being vītarāga (wholly free of attachment), is unmoved by it; the contrast between celestial allure and the dispassionate Conqueror is itself the devotional point — worldly splendour worships what has transcended worldly splendour.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Chanting this verse, the reciter visualises even the radiant heavenly women streaming to bow before the Jina, and so reorients their own attention: away from clinging to sense-beauty and toward the serene, desireless ideal the beauty is honouring. The rolling -iāāhiṁ rhyme also aids a steady, meditative cadence.

crossReferences.text: Bṛhad Ajitaśānti Stava (Smaraṇa 9) — parallel devasundarī tableaux
crossReferences.source: Vīragaṇin / Nava Smaraṇa recensions, Anchalgachchha ed.

crossReferences.text: Continuation in the immediately following verse (girdle-bells, anklets, jewelled armlets of the same women)
crossReferences.source: Ajitaśānti Stava v.27, Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams; Apte)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots underlying the Prakrit tokens: ambara, antara, vicaraṇa, lalita, haṁsa, vadhū, gāminī, pīna, śroṇi, stana, śālinī, sakala, kamala, dala, locana.

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (H. D. T. Sheth, Comprehensive Prakrit Dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit forms and senses: soṇi, thaṇa, loaṇa, vahu, gāmiṇī, viyaraṇa, and the fem. instrumental-plural ending -āhiṁ.

sources.title: Ajitaśānti Stava recensions and Jain stotra anthologies
sources.consultedFor: Verse text and reading of the devasundarī passage (vv.26–31); confirmation of the instrumental-epithet construction within the assembly tableau.

notes: Orthography preserved verbatim, including the cascading doubled-vowel instrumental ending -આઆહિં (-iāāhiṁ) repeated across all four pādas and the conjoined compounds સોણિથણ (soṇithaṇa) and કમલદલ (kamaladala). The four epithets are all feminine instrumental plurals with no finite verb in this line; the verb of veneration (the women bowing at the Jina's feet) is supplied from the surrounding devasundarī stanzas, so the verse is syntactically continuous with its neighbours. Some recensions read વિયારણિઆહિં / single-vowel instrumentals; the book's doubled-vowel forms are kept. 'soṇi' (Skt. śroṇi) is rendered 'hips/loins'; a minority gloss takes it loosely as 'fair', but 'hips' is preferred here for the parallel with 'thaṇa' (breasts).
